segments.s298-g65: چو is the subordinating conjunction “when.” خورشید is the subject “the sun,” followed by the temporal adverb فردا “tomorrow.” بر آرد is a literary verbal phrase: بر + آرد (آوردن) here means “raise/bring up,” with آرد a classical third‑person present/future form (instead of modern خواهد آورد or می‌آورد). درفش is the direct object “banner/standard.”
segments.s298-g66: درفشان functions as an adjectival/participial descriptor “shining, glittering” (or the shining standard); کند is a simple present/future third‑person singular “does/makes.” روی چرخ بنفش is a noun phrase 'the face (روی) of the charkh (چرخ, here = sky/heaven) described as purple (بنفش)'; کند takes that phrase as what is being made to gleam/shine.
segments.s298-g67: This is a coordinated list of subjects: من و خسرو و دشتِ آوردگاه — “I and Khosrow and the plain (field) of the battlefield.” The conjunction و links the nouns; دشت آوردگاه is a compound noun phrase meaning the plain/field that is the arena of combat.
segments.s298-g68: برانگیزم is first‑person singular present/future of برانگیختن “to rouse/raise/rouse into action” (literary form, without an auxiliary); although the prior segment lists multiple subjects, classical Persian often retains a first‑person singular verb when ‘من’ is included. از here marks the source/target relation (in this context “against/from” the following group). شاه is ‘the Shah/king.’ گرد in this context functions as ‘host/group/hostile band’ (a meaning not listed in the provided senses), and سیاه is the adjective “black,” modifying گرد.
segments.s298-g57: بدو گفت is a light prepositional + verb sequence meaning “he said to him” (به + او contracted to بدو + گفت). فردا is the temporal adverb “tomorrow.” بدین رزمگاه = ب + دین + رزمگاه functions as a locative/Directional phrase “in/to this battlefield” (بدین = “to/at this” + رزمگاه “battlefield”). Word order here is verb phrase (بدو گفت) then time (فردا) then place (بدین رزمگاه).
segments.s298-g58: ز افگنده مردان نیابند راه: ز introduces an ablative/reason sense “from/after”; افگنده is a participle meaning “cast down/scattered/laid low” describing مردان (“men”). نیابند is a negative present/optative third‑person plural form of یافتن ‘to find’ meaning “they do not find / will not find.” راه is the noun “path/way.” The clause thus says the scattered or routed men will not find a way (to escape).
segments.s298-g59: به شیده = a prepositional phrase meaning “to/for the shideh (the love‑struck one)” (به + شیده). چنین گفت = “he said thus/so” (چنین “thus” + گفت “said”). کز بامداد uses کز (= از / “from”), introducing the temporal starting point “from dawn.” The clause sets up a reported command introduced by کز بامداد.
segments.s298-g60: مکن is the prohibitive “do not.” تا دو روز = “for two days” (تا here has a durational/temporal sense). ای پسر is a vocative “O son” addressed within the command. جنگ یاد literally “war‑remembrance” (یاد used as ‘mention/remembrance’) combined with مکن yields the prohibition “do not mention/recall the war.” Word order places the negation first (مکن) followed by the time span and the object (جنگ یاد) with a vocative inserted.
segments.s298-g61: بدین رزم = “in/at this battle” (بدین = to/at this + رزم “battle”). بشکست is a past, third‑person form meaning “broke/was broken.” گویی is a parenthetical adverb “it seems / as if.” دلم is دل + enclitic م “my” (my heart). Persian permits the verb before its subject here, so literally “broke, it seems, my heart.”
segments.s298-g62: بر آنم که = an idiomatic predication meaning “I am of that (i.e. I am resolved) that …” (بر آنم = “I am inclined/intent”). که introduces the subordinate clause. دل را = “the heart” with را as the definite/object marker. ز تن = “from the body” (ز = from, تن = body). بگسلم is the first‑person (subjunctive/future‑oriented) form of گسستن “to separate/tear,” here “I will / I would tear/sever.” Together: “I am determined that I will tear the heart from the body.”
segments.s298-g63: پسر گفت = “the youth said” (subject + verb). کای is the vocative interjection “O.” شاه ترکان و چین = a title apposition “king of the Turks and (of) China” (ترکان and چین joined by و). The vocative (کای) + title addresses the ruler directly.
segments.s298-g64: دل خویش را = “your own heart/the heart of yourself” (دل = heart; خویش = reflexive/own; را = object marker). بد مکن = negative imperative “do not make/turn (it) bad” (بد = bad; مکن = do not). روز کین = “the day of vengeance” (روز = day; کین = vengeance/grudge). Altogether: “Do not embitter/trouble your heart on the day of vengeance.”
segments.s298-g17: 'که را' is a relative/indefinite 'who' with the object/definite marker 'را' placed after it (poetic word order) meaning 'whoever'; 'پشت‌گرمی' is a noun 'backing, support' and 'به یزدان' marks the source/agent 'by God' (instrumental/benefactive). The copula 'بود' is past (or general-stative) 'was/is', so the clause means 'whoever had God's support'.
segments.s298-g18: همیشه 'always' modifies the whole predicate; 'دل و بخت' is a compound subject ('heart and fortune'); 'خندان' is a predicative adjective 'smiling/cheerful'; 'بود' is the copula. Word order is adverb — subject — adjective — copula, a common poetic ordering meaning 'his heart and fortune were always cheerful'.
segments.s298-g19: A rhythmic list joined by و: 'بر و بوم و گنج و سپاهت' names possessions (realm/land, home, treasure, and your army). The predicate 'مراست' (= 'مرا + است') functions as 'is mine / belongs to me' (poetic fused form). Note the possessive suffix -ت on 'سپاه' = 'your army'.
segments.s298-g20: 'همان' here is emphatic 'that very / likewise'; 'تخت' 'throne' and 'زرین کلاهت' 'your golden cap/crown' form the second coordinated possession; again 'مراست' is the predicate 'is mine'. Adjective 'زرین' qualifies 'کلاهت' (possessive -ت = your).
segments.s298-g21: 'پشنگ' is the proper-name subject; 'آمد' is simple past 'came'. 'و' links clauses; 'خواست' is past 'asked/wanted/demanded'; 'از من' 'from me' marks the source/complainant; 'نبرد' is the noun 'battle/contest' as the object of 'خواست'. Grammatically: 'Pashang came and asked me for a battle.'
segments.s298-g22: A compressed, poetic noun phrase describing the fought/received opponent or the nature of the contest: 'زره‌دار' is an 'armored man/armored one'; 'بی لشکر و دار' uses 'بی' = 'without' to list what he lacked ('without army and without home/possessions'); the final 'و برد' is terse — here 'برد' is best read as the nominal 'victory' / 'gain' in the catalogue. The whole fragment is a compact, paratactic listing common in epic diction.
segments.s298-g23: 'سپیده‌دمان' is an adverbial time phrase 'at daybreak'; 'هست' is the copula 'is/exists'; 'مهمان من' is 'a guest of mine' (possessive). Word order places the time first for emphasis: 'At dawn there is a guest of mine.'
segments.s298-g24: 'به خنجر' uses به in an instrumental/agentive sense 'with/by a dagger'; 'ببیند' (subjunctive/indicative form of دیدن) here functions as 3rd-person 'he will see/he shall see' (poetic future/optative sense); 'سرافشان' is a compound adjective 'head-scattering' (literally 'head-scattered' — in epic poetry implying blood-spraying or hair dishevelment) describing the speaker; 'من' is the object 'me'. The clause thus means 'he will see me, my head scattering, by a dagger.'
segments.s298-g41: جهاندارت is the subject 'the sovereign' (جهاندار) with a poetic/archaic ending -ت; انگیخت is the simple past (3rd singular) of انگیختن used intransitively here meaning 'rose/stood up'; از انجمن is a postpositional phrase 'from the assembly' (preposition از + noun). Word order is subject–verb–prepositional phrase, typical of Ferdowsi's epic style.
segments.s298-g42: ستودانت functions as a noun/possessive phrase 'your praise' (ستودان with the enclitic -ت indicating possession/object); ایدر is the adverb 'here'; بود is the past copula 'was'; هم کفن is the predicate 'also a shroud' (هم = 'also', کفن = 'shroud'). The clause is: 'Your praise here was also a shroud.'
segments.s298-g43: گزند is the noun 'harm'; آیدت is the impersonal/experiential form 'comes to you' (آید + enclitic ت = 'to you'); زان 'from that' is a prepositional/demonstrative particle pointing back to a specific head; سر بی‌گزند = 'the head (that is) unharmed', where بی‌گزند is a postpositive adjective. The clause means 'Harm will come to you from that unharmed head.'
segments.s298-g44: که introduces a relative clause 'which' (i.e. 'that head which ...'); از تن بریدند is a past-tense clause 'they cut from the body' (بریدند = 3rd plural past); چون گوسفند is a simile 'like a sheep' modifying how it was cut. The relative clause follows the noun it qualifies, a standard classical order.
segments.s298-g45: بیامد is the past tense 'came' (3rd singular); دمان قارن here functions as a compound proper name/title (Daman Qaran) — grammatically it is the subject; از نزد شاه is a prepositional phrase 'from the presence/side of the king.' The clause literally: 'Daman Qaran came from (out of) the king's presence.'
segments.s298-g46: به نزد is a fixed combination 'to the presence of' (به = to/toward, نزد = in the presence of); یکی is the indefinite 'a certain' (here 'a certain man'); آن درفش سیاه is a noun phrase 'that black standard/banner' (آن = that, درفش = standard, سیاه = black). Together: 'to the presence of a certain one, that black standard' — i.e. he approached a certain man who bore that black banner.
segments.s298-g47: سخن هرچ = 'whatever words' (سخن = speech/words; هرچ = whatever/whatever was); بشنید is past tense 'he heard'; با او بگفت = 'he told (it) to him' (با marking the indirect object, او = him, بگفت = said/told). The sequence presents hearing followed by reporting: 'He heard whatever words and told them to him.'
segments.s298-g48: نماند is the past 'remained' used impersonally here; ایچ = 'nothing' (so نماند ایچ = 'nothing remained'); نیک و بد = 'good and bad' listed together; اندر نهفت = 'hidden within' (اندر = in/within, نهفت = hidden/concealed). The whole clause: 'Nothing, neither good nor bad, remained hidden inside.'
segments.s298-g49: بشد is an archaic past/becoming verb meaning 'became' (verb before predicate). شیده is a predicate adjective meaning 'smitten / love-struck' describing the subject; نزدیک + افراسیاب is a prepositional/locative phrase 'near Afrasiab' where نزدیک (adverb/adjective) precedes the proper name.
segments.s298-g50: دلش is دل + enclitic ش 'his heart'. چون here means 'as if' introducing a simile. بر آتش نهاده is a perfect participial construction ('placed upon the fire') with نهاده as a past participle; the copula is omitted. کباب is used metaphorically as the resulting state ('roasted/seared like kebab').
segments.s298-g51: ببد is an archaic past-form meaning 'became/was turned' (predicate verb). شاه ترکان is a nominal phrase 'the king of the Turks' (شاه = king, ترکان = the Turks). ز here marks cause/point of reference 'at/from the reply', and دژم is an adjective 'grim / sullen' modifying پاسخ.
segments.s298-g52: غمی گشت is a verbless-resultive pattern rendered with گشت 'became' — 'a grief came to be' = 'sorrow arose'. و links clauses. برزد is an archaic verb meaning 'fell/occurred' (here 'struck/occurred'); یکی تیز دم is the noun phrase 'one sharp moment/breath' (یکی = one, تیز = sharp, دم = moment), the event specified as what occurred.
segments.s298-g53: از آن خواب = 'from that dream' (از marking source/reference). کز is the fused relative 'that/which' introducing a clause modifying خواب. روزگار دراز is a noun+adjective 'long time' (دراز modifying روزگار). Together: 'from that dream which was of long time' = 'that long-ago dream'.
segments.s298-g54: بدید is past 'saw' (the verb of the clause). و 'and' links the second clause. ز هر کس همی داشت راز literally 'from every person he used to have a secret' — ز marks source/target ('from/with each person'), همی gives habitual/continuous sense in past, and داشت (had) takes راز (secret) as object: 'he kept/held secrets regarding every man' or 'had confidences from everyone.'
segments.s298-g55: سرش is 'his head' (سر + enclitic ش). گشت is past 'became/turned' and takes گردان ('revolving, turned') as predicate: 'his head became dizzy/turned about.' و links to دل پرنهیب where دل 'heart' with adjective پرنهیب (پر- = full of + نهیب 'awe/dread/rebuke') means 'his heart was full of dread/was alarmed.'
segments.s298-g56: بدانست is 'he knew it / perceived' and introduces the content clause. کامد is an archaic imperfective/present-like verb 'comes / will come' (here 'was coming' or 'would come'). به is the preposition 'to/toward' marking the direction/result, and تنگی نشیب is a compound noun phrase 'narrow descent' or 'a tight/oppressive downturn' (تنگی = narrowness/distress, نشیب = descent). The clause therefore means 'he realized that a narrow descent/strait was coming.'
segments.s298-g9: نخواهم is the negative first-person present/future form of خواستن used here as 'I will not/wish not' (ن‑ as negation + خواه + ـم). ز is the poetic preposition 'from'. The nouns 'اسب و دینار و گنج' are simply coordinated by و ('and') and are the things the speaker refuses to take; the verb (نخواهم) comes before the list for metrical/poetic order.
segments.s298-g10: که introduces an explanatory/causal clause ('for/since'). بر کس is an idiomatic prepositional phrase meaning 'to/for anyone' (بر = to/toward; کس = anyone). نماند is the negative 3rd-person singular of ماندن ('does not remain / is not left'). سرای سپنج is a nominal phrase (house/abode + سپنج); note that the ezāfe is omitted for metrical reasons, so the second noun qualifies the first.
segments.s298-g11: به زور functions as an instrumental/ablative phrase 'by the might/force'. جهان‌آفرین is a compound noun 'world-creator' placed before کردگار (a stylistic apposition), so the whole phrase means 'by the might of the Creator of the world.' The preposition به is used in the instrumental sense 'by/through'.
segments.s298-g12: The second به repeats the instrumental 'by' in parallel. دیهیم is an adjective/noun 'rural, rustic' that here qualifies کاوس (Kay Kāvus), and پروردگار is an appositive/title 'the nurturer / lord'. The sequence can be read as a pair of instrumental phrases: 'by ... the Creator' and 'by ... the lord who reared Ka'us.'
segments.s298-g13: که here introduces a clause ('that' / 'so that'); چندان is an adverb 'to that extent / so long'. نمانم is 1st-person negative present/subjunctive of ماندن ('I will not remain / I shall not stay') with the implicit subject 'I'. شما را uses را as an object/topic marker meaning 'with/among you' (classical usage), and زمان is the noun 'time'. Word order places the verb at the end, typical of Persian clauses.
segments.s298-g14: که begins a subordinate temporal clause ('when'). بر گل is a prepositional phrase 'upon the flower' (بر = on/upon; گل = flower). جهد here functions in an archaic/poetic way as a verbal idea 'strives / exerts itself' (though the provided sense is 'effort'); تندباد خزان is a compound 'the gale of autumn.' The clause describes the occasion/time at which the speaker will not remain.
segments.s298-g15: بدان is an older compound equivalent to به آن ('to/for that') and here marks the thing referred to. خواسته is a noun 'wish/desire'. نیست is the negative copula 'is not/there is not'. ما را uses را to mark the experiencer/possessor ('for us'); نیاز is the noun 'need'. Together the segment says 'We have no need for that desire.'
segments.s298-g16: که here gives the causal/explanatory reason ('because'). از جور و بیدادی is a prepositional phrase 'out of/from injustice and cruelty' with و linking the two nouns. آمد is the past-tense verb 'came/arrived', and فراز is an adverbial/nominal particle 'up/on high' (i.e. 'rose up'). The clause explains why there is no need for that wish.
segments.s298-g33: از آن پس is a temporal phrase meaning 'from then on' (از + demonstrative آن + پس 'after'). یلان را is the definite direct object (را marks the object) with همه 'all/everyone' qualifying یلان; همگروه is an attributive/noun 'fellow-companions' placed after همه. Word order is typical older Persian S‑N‑O: temporal phrase first, then the object with را before the verb that follows.
segments.s298-g34: به جنگ اندر آریم is the verbal phrase: اندر (archaic 'into/within') plus the verb آریم (an older/poetic form meaning 'we bring/put/send' — first‑person plural optative/subjunctive/imperative sense 'let us / we shall set'). In Persian the object (یلان را) precedes the verb. بر سان کوه is a simile: سان = 'like/as' with کوه 'mountain'; بر is a preposition/deictic linking the simile ('upon/as a mountain').
segments.s298-g35: چو این گفت باشی is a temporal clause 'when/after you have said this.' In older Persian the perfect participle could appear as گفت (instead of گفته) followed by the auxiliary باشی to mark an anterior action ('have said'). به شیده بگوی is an imperative instruction 'tell Shideh' (به + name as indirect object; بگوی is an imperative/optative form of گفتن here).
segments.s298-g36: که‌ای here functions as the vocative particle 'که ای' = 'O' (addressing someone). کم خرد is a compound adjective 'little‑witted / small of wisdom' (کم modifies خرد), followed by مهتر 'chief/leader' in direct address, and کامجوی 'one who seeks desire/pleasure' as a descriptive epithet. The sequence is an archaic vocative insult: 'O small‑minded chief, seeker of desire.'
segments.s298-g37: نه‌تنها ... is the correlative 'not only ...' construction. تو ایدر 'you here' (ایدِر = ایدر = here) is the topical subject; به کام آمدی is an idiom 'to have one's desire fulfilled' (به + کام + آمدی). The verb آمدی is past/perfect 'you came/you attained' used idiomatically.
segments.s298-g38: نه ... آمدی continues the negative contrast: نه introduces the second negative clause. بر جستن is a verbal noun phrase 'for the seeking / to seek' (بر indicates purpose/toward), ننگ و نام 'shame and name' here 'shame and fame' are the objects of that seeking, and آمدی is the main verb ('you came' / 'you came for the purpose of'). The whole clause means 'nor did you come to seek shame or fame.'
segments.s298-g39: نه از بهر ... is an adverbial negation 'not for the sake of / not because of.' از بهر (a fixed older prepositional phrase) means 'for/on account of'; پیغام افراسیاب is the noun phrase 'Afrasiab's message' serving as the object of that preposition.
segments.s298-g40: که introduces the explanatory clause (here 'but/because'), کردار بد کرد is a catenation where کردار 'deed/behavior' is followed by بد 'bad' and the past verb کرد 'did' (i.e. 'he acted ill'). بر تو شتاب places the manner/target: بر تو 'toward you' and شتاب 'haste' (i.e. 'with haste'), so the clause explains the true reason: his conduct toward you was ill and rash.
segments.s298-g1: بفرمود is an honorific past verb meaning 'he commanded/said'; تا introduces a subordinate clause of purpose/command ('that' / 'so that'); قارن is the proper name (the addressee); نیک‌خواه is an epithet/attribute attached to the name meaning 'well-wisher' or 'good‑hearted'. The phrase is verb + subordinate marker + noun phrase (no explicit copula).
segments.s298-g2: شود is the subjunctive/optative/preterite sense of شدن here meaning 'become / (let him) be / return' (with تا it marks the desired action). باز functions as an adverb ('back/again' — here implying 'return'). و = 'and'. پاسخ گزارد is an idiom 'to make/give answer' (گزارد is an older narrative-form verb 'to place/make' used here as 'make/give'); ز شاه is a poetic prepositional phrase marking the other party (here: 'to/for the king' — archaic usage of ز). Together: '(let him) return and give an answer to the king.'
segments.s298-g3: که here introduces an explanatory/causal clause ('for/because'); این کار ما = 'this affair of ours' (demonstrative + noun + possessive). دیر و دشوار گشت is a compound predicate where گشت (past of گشتن) = 'became', and دیر (long/protracted) and دشوار (difficult) are predicative adjectives describing the change.
segments.s298-g4: سخنها (plural 'words, speeches') is the subject; ز اندازه اندر گذشت is an idiomatic verbless/compound expression meaning 'passed beyond measure' — ز here is the poetic preposition 'beyond/from', اندازه = 'measure/extent', اندر = 'in/within' (classical placement), and گذشت is past tense 'passed/exceeded'.
segments.s298-g5: هنر یافته is a compound participial phrase 'skill-acquired' (= 'experienced/skilled'); it modifies مرد ('man'), so هنر یافته مرد = 'the man who has acquired skill'. سنگی is an adjective attached to مرد (mord + سنگی = 'stone-like/stone-hearted man'). به جنگ is a prepositional phrase 'into/for battle' that sets the domain of the following verb.
segments.s298-g6: نجوید is the negative present/archaic form 'does not seek' (ن- + جوید), so 'does not seek/desire'; گه = 'sometimes/occasionally'; رزم = 'battle/combat'; چندین درنگ = 'many a pause/delay' (چندین = several, درنگ = pause/delay). The line is elliptical: 'he does not seek battle; sometimes the fighting is held up for many pauses.'
segments.s298-g7: کنون = 'now'. تا here introduces a wish/optative clause (classical usage: 'may ...'), so the sequence 'کنون تا' launches a benediction or hope. خداوند خورشید و ماه is a grand epithet 'Lord of the Sun and Moon' (i.e., God or the sovereign), the subject of the wish.
segments.s298-g8: که is the relative/interrogative 'whom' here (object of the verb); را is the definite/object marker emphasizing that object; شاد دارد is 'keeps/makes (someone) happy' (دارد = 'has/keeps', here present/wishive sense thanks to تا). بدین رزمگاه (classical 'ب' + 'این' = 'in/at this') means 'in this battlefield' — note the tokenization in the word list splits بدین into 'ب' + 'دین' (the second part should be این 'this'); read together as the adverbial phrase 'in this battlefield.'
segments.s298-g25: کسی را نخواهم ز ایران سپاه is a negated object clause: کسی (indefinite pronoun) marked by the object marker را is the thing being refused ("no one"). نخواهم is the first-person negative of خواستن (here "I will not/would not want/send"). ز ایران سپاه (literally "from Iran army") specifies the source/group (poetic word order; more prosaic: سپاهِ ایران).
segments.s298-g26: که introduces a relative clause modifying کسی ("who/that"). با او is a prepositional phrase "with him." بگردد is a third-person singular verb form of گردیدن (here meaning to go/roam/come about) describing accompanying movement, and به آوردگاه places that movement "to/into the battlefield."
segments.s298-g27: A coordinated noun phrase: من و شیده و دشت و شمشیر تیز lists the agents/elements. شیده is a poetic noun/adjective meaning an obsessed/maddened one; دشت is "the plain"; شمشیر تیز is a noun + postpositive adjective (common in poetry) meaning "sharp sword."
segments.s298-g28: برآرم is a first-person verb (I will raise/bring up) built with the prefix بر- plus the present/subjunctive ending; به فرجام is an adverbial phrase "for/to the end/at last." از او رستخیز is the object phrase: رستخیز (uprising/resurrection) is produced "from him." Word order is poetic: verb then adverbial then object.
segments.s298-g29: گر is the conditional "if." ایدون is an archaic/poetic temporal adverb (here signalling ‘henceforth/at this time’). که introduces the clause whose predicate is پیروز گردم: پیروز (an adjective) precedes the verb گردم (first-person form of گشتن, 'become'), and به جنگ specifies the arena "in battle."
segments.s298-g30: نسازم is the first-person negative of ساختن here meaning "I will not make/do/act (so)." بر این‌سان is a prepositional phrase "in this way/so." که گفتی درنگ is a relative/content clause: که links to the manner, گفتی is the second-person past "you said," and درنگ is the noun "delay/hesitation"—i.e., 'I will not do as you said (to delay).' Note: گفتی here is a verb form ("you said").
segments.s298-g31: مبارز خروشان is a noun phrase where مبارز (fighters) is modified postpositively by خروشان (roaring/tumultuous). کنیم is the first-person plural verb "we make/do" (we will cause). از دو روی is a prepositional phrase "from two faces/sides" meaning 'from both fronts.' The verb follows the phrase as is typical in poetic word order.
segments.s298-g32: ز خون uses the poetic preposition ز (from/because of) + خون (blood) to indicate cause. دشت گردد is the copular/result clause: دشت (the plain) + گردد (becomes) yields 'the plain becomes.' پر از رنگ و بوی is a predicative phrase: پر (full [of]) + از introduces the contents (رنگ و بوی = color and scent). Overall: 'because of blood, the plain becomes full of color and scent.'

sentences.s298-s33: When the sun tomorrow raises the banner, the shining standard will make the purple face of the heavens gleam.
sentences.s298-s34: I and Khosrow and the plain of the battlefield will rouse (ourselves) against the Shah’s black host.
sentences.s298-s29: He said to him, “Tomorrow, in this battlefield, the routed men will not find a way.”
sentences.s298-s30: He said thus to the love-struck one: “From dawn, do not mention the war for two days, O son.”
sentences.s298-s31: At this battle my heart was broken, it seems; I am bent on tearing the heart from the body.
sentences.s298-s32: The youth said, “O king of the Turks and China, do not trouble your heart on the day of vengeance.”
sentences.s298-s9: Whoever had God's backing, his heart and fortune were always cheerful.
sentences.s298-s10: Your land and home and treasure and army are mine; likewise your throne and your golden cap are mine.
sentences.s298-s11: Pashang came and demanded battle from me; (he) an armored man, without an army or home, and victory.
sentences.s298-s12: At daybreak my guest is (present); with a dagger he will see me, my head scattering (i.e., my head spouting/splattered).
sentences.s298-s21: The sovereign rose from the assembly; your praise here was also a shroud.
sentences.s298-s22: Harm will come to you from that unharmed head which they cut off from the body like a sheep.
sentences.s298-s23: Daman Qaran came from the king's presence to the presence of a certain man bearing that black standard.
sentences.s298-s24: Whatever words he heard he told him; nothing — good or bad — remained hidden inside.
sentences.s298-s25: He became smitten and drew near Afrasiab; his heart, as if set upon the fire, was seared like kebab.
sentences.s298-s26: The Turkic king grew sullen at the stern reply; a grief arose, and in one sharp instant it struck.
sentences.s298-s27: He had seen that dream long ago, and he held a secret about every person.
sentences.s298-s28: His head turned and reeled and his heart became full of dread; he perceived that a narrow descent was coming.
sentences.s298-s5: I will take no horse, dinar, or treasure from you, for the sponge-house does not remain to anyone.
sentences.s298-s6: By the might of the world's Creator, by the Lord who fostered Ka'us.
sentences.s298-s7: that I will not remain with you so long a time, when the autumn gale exerts itself upon the flower.
sentences.s298-s8: We have no need of that wish, for out of injustice and cruelty there rose up (a thing).
sentences.s298-s17: From then on we marshaled all the heroes together and sent them into battle like a mountain.
sentences.s298-s18: When you have said this, tell Shideh: 'O small-minded chief, seeker of desire.'
sentences.s298-s19: Not only did you here gain your desire, nor did you come to seek shame or fame.
sentences.s298-s20: Not for Afrasiab's message; rather, because his conduct toward you was bad and hasty.
sentences.s298-s1: He ordered that Qaren, the well-wisher, return and give an answer to the king.
sentences.s298-s2: For this matter of ours became prolonged and difficult; the words passed beyond measure.
sentences.s298-s3: A man who has acquired skill does not seek battle; at times combat is delayed many a while.
sentences.s298-s4: Now, may the Lord of Sun and Moon keep whoever he delights happy in this battlefield.
sentences.s298-s13: I will have no one of Iran's army who would go with him to the battlefield.
sentences.s298-s14: At the end I and the maddened one, the plain and the sharp sword will raise up an uprising because of him.
sentences.s298-s15: If henceforth I become victorious in battle, I will not act in the manner you suggested by pausing (I will not delay).
sentences.s298-s16: We will make the fighters roar from both sides; from blood the plain will become filled with color and scent.
